result:
The Art of Wandering: In Praise of Getting Lost

There's something profoundly human about the act of wandering. In an age where GPS guides our every step and algorithms predict our every desire, the simple act of getting lost has become almost revolutionary. Yet it's in these moments of displacement, when we don't quite know where we are or where we're going, that we often find ourselves most fully.

I remember the first time I truly got lost. Not the panicked kind of lost where you're late for something important, but the delicious kind where time becomes irrelevant and every corner promises a new discovery. It was in a foreign city whose name I won't mention, because the specific place matters less than the feeling. The morning had started with a plan—museums, monuments, the usual tourist checklist—but somewhere between the second coffee and a wrong turn down an alley that smelled of fresh bread, the plan dissolved.

The alley led to a small square where old men played chess on stone tables worn smooth by decades of similar games. One of them looked up and nodded, a simple acknowledgment that said nothing and everything. I nodded back and kept walking, drawn by the sound of a fountain I couldn't see. This is the thing about wandering: it engages senses we usually ignore. When you know where you're going, you look without seeing, move without feeling the ground beneath your feet. But when you're lost, every detail becomes significant. The way light falls through a particular archway. The pattern of cobblestones that suggests great age. The sudden shift in temperature that tells you you're near water.

Our ancestors were wanderers by necessity. They followed seasons and herds, reading landscapes like texts, finding their way by stars and the flight patterns of birds. We've traded this deep knowledge for convenience, and mostly that's a good trade. I wouldn't want to truly depend on my ability to navigate by the stars. But something in our DNA still responds to the call of the unknown path, the road that branches where no road should be.

Writers and artists have always known this. Baudelaire wrote about the flâneur, the passionate wanderer of city streets who finds poetry in the random encounters of urban life. Virginia Woolf took long walks through London, letting her mind wander as her feet did, gathering impressions that would later crystallize into prose. The Situationists of 1960s Paris developed the concept of the dérive—the drift—a technique of rapid passage through varied ambiances, letting the terrain dictate the journey.

But wandering isn't just for artists or philosophers. It's a fundamental human need that we've increasingly engineered out of our lives. Children understand this instinctively. Watch a child walk anywhere and you'll see the true art of wandering: the sudden fascination with a crack in the sidewalk, the imperative need to balance on every raised edge, the magnetic pull of puddles. We train this out of them, teaching efficiency and purpose, forgetting that the most profound discoveries often come from inefficiency and purposelessness.

The digital age has created new forms of wandering, of course. We speak of surfing the web, of falling down rabbit holes of hyperlinks, of getting lost in the infinite scroll. But there's a crucial difference between digital wandering and physical wandering. Online, algorithms track our every click, building profiles, predicting preferences, creating echo chambers that feel like exploration but are actually elaborate mazes designed to keep us in place. Physical wandering, by contrast, offers genuine randomness, real chance encounters, unexpected juxtapositions that no algorithm would think to suggest.

I've found that the best wandering happens in that liminal state between familiar and foreign. In your own city, but in a neighborhood you've never explored. In a place where you speak enough of the language to ask basic questions but not enough to eavesdrop comfortably. This slight discomfort sharpens attention. You become porous to your surroundings, absorbing details you'd otherwise filter out.

There's also something to be said for wandering in nature, though it requires more caution than urban wandering. The stakes are higher—genuine danger lurks in truly getting lost in wilderness. But the rewards are proportional. Away from human-made paths, following deer trails or the curve of a stream, you enter into an older conversation. The landscape speaks in a language pre-dating words: the way trees lean indicates prevailing winds, the plants that grow speak of water and soil, the sudden silence might mean a predator is near.

Modern life militates against wandering. We schedule our days in fifteen-minute increments, optimize our routes, measure our steps. Productivity culture tells us that all movement must have purpose, all time must be accounted for. But wandering resists this logic. It insists that not all who wander are lost—that sometimes being lost is precisely the point.

The pandemic, for all its horrors, reminded many of us of the value of wandering. Confined to our neighborhoods, we discovered streets we'd never noticed, parks we'd driven past a thousand times without seeing. The simple act of walking without destination became a form of meditation, a way of marking time when all days blurred together.

As I write this, I'm planning my next wander. Not planning in the sense of mapping a route, but preparing for routelessness. Charging my phone (for emergencies) but vowing not to consult it. Choosing comfortable shoes. Clearing an afternoon of obligations. The anticipation itself is part of the pleasure—the knowledge that in a few hours I'll be somewhere I can't currently imagine, seeing things I don't yet know exist.

This is what wandering gives us: the gift of surprise in a world that increasingly resists it. The reminder that we are bodies moving through space, animals with legs and lungs and curious eyes. The proof that the world is still larger than our knowledge of it, that there are still discoveries to be made, even if they're only personal ones.

So I encourage you: get lost. Turn off your GPS. Take the bus to an unfamiliar stop. Follow your feet instead of your head. Let yourself drift. In a world of constant connectivity and surveillance, the ability to disappear for a while, to be unreachable and untracked, following nothing but your own curiosity—this is a form of resistance, a reclaiming of an essential human freedom.

The city, the forest, the suburb—they're all waiting to be discovered. Not by tourists or travelers, but by wanderers. By you, if you're willing to get just a little bit lost.
